case_name:
39.	InPCC vChand6the Tribunal
applied the principles as statedin B v Medical

summary.brief:
39.	InPCC vChand6the Tribunal
applied the principles as statedin B v Medical. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: The client was at the time (2011) a 15 year old young woman.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The client’s mother alleged that the counsellor that she was seeing at Waves, who turned out to be Mr Joseph, “ told her that she is not ADHD and told her to stop taking her medication ”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal finds that Mr Joseph has not complied with his obligations concerning adequate note taking, certain aspects of professional supervision, and proper recording of his professional supervision, but these are each of themselves not of sufficient severity to warrant disciplinary sanction.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal finds that this Particular of both Charges is made out.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal is of the view that those Particulars found to have been made out and which justify disciplinary sanctions are best found as malpractice rather than as bringing discredit to the profession of psychology.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Waves was an organisation which works with young people in the New Plymouth area.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Accordingly those Particulars of the Charges which are found to have been made out apply to Charge No 1 and not to Charge No 2 and that latter Charge is dismissed.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The notes of Mr Joseph did not achieve that. The note-taking aspects of the Charges are found by the Tribunal to amount to negligence as that expression is interpreted but are not of themselves found of sufficient severity to warrant disciplinary sanction.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal finds secondly that, in respect of the aspects concerning PTSD, these arose so late in the proceedings that they need not be considered further; but in any event there appears insufficient evidence that the advice and actions by Mr Joseph were inappropriate.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Tribunal finds that Mr Joseph has not complied with his obligations concerning adequate note taking, certain aspects of professional supervision, and proper recording of his professional supervision, but these are each of themselves not of sufficient severity to warrant disciplinary sanction.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient was attending Waves for psychological support, had ADHD and was on medication.

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

requestables.name: Psychological Consultation Notes
requestables.description: Notes from consultations with Mr Joseph

reveal.result_summary: Notes were inadequate and not comprehensive
reveal.clinical_significance: Inadequate documentation can lead to poor continuity of care
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted better record-keeping practices

timeline_phases.name: Psychological Care Phase
timeline_phases.description: Period during which the patient was under the care of Mr Joseph

decision_points.prompt: What should be done regarding the documentation of psychological sessions?

options.description: Ensure comprehensive and continuous documentation of all sessions
options.clinical_reasoning: Comprehensive documentation is essential for continuity of care and legal compliance
Leads to inadequate record-keeping and potential for miscommunication

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant cited time and resource pressures

expected_action_court.description: Comprehensive documentation should have been maintained

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Inadequate documentation does not meet professional standards
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Comprehensive notes should have been kept

patient_outcome.description: Patient did not receive appropriate ADHD management

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to consult with mental health team
Causation established - inadequate management of ADHD

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to consult with mental health team
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Consultation would have ensured appropriate management

allegations.allegation: Failure to consult with mental health team

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by inadequate note-taking
